sql · backend logic · er modeling

A.F.V. Travel

teamVaishnavi Kusumba, Alyssa Jacoby, Frances Huffman,
roleBackend/Database Engineer
skillsSQL, ER Modeling, BCNF Normalization
ambitionProve the system could survive real bookings, not just a demo.
the problem

What would it take to build a travel booking system that could actually survive real bookings?

the process

We started with an ER diagram mapping how customers, employees, packages, and every piece of a package (hotels, flights, transportation, activities) relate to each other. From there we wrote DDL for eleven tables and normalized the schema up through BCNF, checking at every step that non-key attributes depended only on their table's primary key and nothing else.

The ER diagram behind the schema — eleven entities, all normalized through BCNF.

On the frontend, we built the whole flow around one idea: search by budget first, browse second. A customer sets what they can spend, and the results page filters flights, hotels, and activities to fit — sortable by category or popularity.

the solution

Customers create an account, set a budget, and get a dashboard of flights, hotels, and activities that actually fit it. Checking items off adds them straight to a package in their cart, and checkout applies a platform discount before completing the (simulated) transaction. Every one of those actions is backed by foreign-key-enforced tables on the back end, so a booking can never reference a flight, hotel, or activity that doesn't actually exist in the system.

127.0.0.1:5000/register

Creating an account.

127.0.0.1:5000/dashboard

Setting a budget before browsing.

127.0.0.1:5000/search

Search results, filtered to fit the budget.

Hotel options within budget.

Activities that can be added to a package.

127.0.0.1:5000/cart

Cart, discount applied, ready to check out.

what i learned

Normalizing a schema on paper is one thing — watching it hold up once a real frontend is actually hitting it with requests is another. Splitting Cart and Cart_Item turned out to be one of our better decisions, since it kept every customer's in-progress booking private and tied only to them. If I rebuilt this, I'd connect it to a real pricing API instead of manually-entered listings, since outdated listings were our biggest limitation.
